title: Chermoula
subtitle: Moroccan herb and spice marinade
excerpt: A vibrant paste of coriander, parsley, garlic, lemon, cumin, paprika, and olive oil, the foundational marinade and sauce of Moroccan cooking. Chermoula goes on fish before grilling, into tagines as the base flavour, and alongside grilled meat as a sauce. It takes five minutes to make and transforms everything it touches.
sourceNotes: Chermoula is the primary marinade of Moroccan cooking, used predominantly for fish though applicable to all proteins. The combination of coriander, parsley, garlic, cumin, and paprika is consistent across regional variations; the proportions and additional ingredients (saffron, preserved lemon, cayenne) vary by household and region. Paula Wolfert's Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco documents several versions.

content.text: Chermoula should be made fresh for best results, though it keeps well. The coriander and parsley provide freshness; the garlic and cumin provide depth; the paprika and lemon provide colour and acidity. These proportions are a starting point, more cumin for a more savoury version, more lemon for a brighter one.

A blender or small food processor gives a smooth paste. Made by hand (finely chopped herbs and crushed garlic) it is coarser but equally good. The difference matters for fish, where a smooth paste adheres better; for dipping bread, the texture is less important.

Combine all ingredients in a small food processor or blender: {{coriander}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{cayenne}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{olive-oil}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Blitz to a coarse paste. Taste and adjust: more lemon for brightness, more cumin for depth, more cayenne for heat. Use immediately or refrigerate in a sealed jar.

items.fix: Add olive oil a tablespoon at a time and pulse. The paste should be spoonable but not runny
Use more leaf than stem in the coriander. Over-blending garlic releases more harsh compounds; pulse rather than blitz continuously
items.cause: Too much coriander stem or over-blended garlic

items.fix: Cumin is the key flavour note. Taste and add more. The lemon should be assertive, not subtle

content.text: Chermoula is the Moroccan cook's starting point. It goes on fish (whole sea bream, monkfish steaks, sardines) before grilling or roasting; it is used as a baste for chicken; it is stirred into tagines at the beginning of cooking. In Morocco every cook has their own version, adjusted to personal taste. Outside Morocco it has become one of the more accessible Moroccan techniques, appearing in restaurant kitchens and food writing as a simple way to add North African flavour to fish and seafood. It is worth making in quantity and keeping a jar in the fridge.
